Stanley, Edward John, Baron Stanley of Alderley and Baron Eddisbury of Win- nington (1802-1869) — British states- man, Whig, M.P.; President of the Board of Trade (1855-58), Postmas- ter-General (1860-66).—425-26, 428

Stead, Jean—French émigré on Jersey, printer.—496

Stein, Maximilian, Baron (1811- 1860)—Austrian army officer; dur- ing the revolution of 1848-49 in Hungary was chief of the General Staff of the revolutionary army; then émigré in Turkey under the name of Ferhad Pasha; fought against Russia in the Caucasus (1857-58).—541-43, 545

Stephen III the Great (d. 1504) — Hospodar of Moldavia (1457- 1504).—85

Stewart, Sir Donald Martin (1824- 1900) — British army officer, later field marshal; took part in suppres- sing the national liberation uprising of 1857-59 in India, and in the second Anglo-Afghan war (1878- 80); commander-in-chief of the British troops in India (1881-85); member of the Council of India (1885-1900).—364



**[p. 249]**

Stock, Franciszek—Polish émigré, of- ficer in the Turkish army.— 539,

Stocken, von—Danish minister (early 18th cent.).—43, 44

Stormont, David Murray, Viscount Stor- mont, Earl of Mansfield (1727- 1796) — British statesman and dip- lomat, Tory; envoy to Paris (1772- 78); Secretary of State for the Southern Department (1779-82).— 32-33, 35

Strabo (63 B.C.-c. A.D. 20) —Greek geographer and historian.—146

Strahan, William (born c. 1808) — British banker, partner of a firm which went bankrupt in June 1855; sentenced to hard labour for finan- cial machinations.—110

Stratford de Redcliffe, Stratford Canning, Viscount (1786-1880) —British dip- lomat, envoy to Constantinople (1810-12, 1825-28, 1841-58).—293, 322, 595

Stroganovs (Stroganoffs)—the family of Russian merchants, later of nobles; known for their efforts to colonise the Urals and Siberia in the 16th- 18th centuries; were close to the Court in the 18th-19th centuries.— 31 Suetonius (Gaius Suetonius Tranquillus) (c. 70-c. 160) — Roman historian and writer, author of The Twelve Caesars.— 164

Sugden, Edward Burtenshaw, Baron St. Leonards (1781-1875) —British lawyer and statesman, Tory; Lord Chancellor (1852).—469

Sugden, Richard—hairdresser, father of the above.—469

Svyatoslav Igorevich (d. 972 or 973) — Grand Duke of Kiev (c. 945-72).— 75, 76

Sze'mere, Bertalan (Bartholomäus) (1812- 1869) — Hungarian politician and journalist; Minister of the Interior (1848) and head of the revolutio- nary government (1849); emigrated after the defeat of the revolution.— 236

T Tahlman—Austrian resident in Con- stantinople in 1736.— 27

Tahmasp II (died c. 1736) —Shah of Persia (1730-32).—177

Talleyrand-Périgord, Charles Maurice, prince de (1754-1838) —French dip- lomat, Foreign Minister (1797-99, 1799-1807, 1814-15); represented France at the Vienna Congress (1814-15).—29, 227, 488, 491, 492, 498

Tao Kuang (1782-1850) — Emperor of China (1821-50).—244-45

Tasso, Torquato (1544-1595) — Italian poet.—477

Terence (Publius Terentius Afer) (c. 190-159 B.C.) —Roman author of comedies.— 55

Terentianus Maurus (2nd half of the 2nd cent.) — Roman grammarian.— 15

Tewfik Bey—see Lapinski, Teofil

Themistocles (c. 525-c. 460 B.C.)— Greek statesman and general of the time of Greco-Persian wars.— 55



**[p. mili]**

Theyls, Willem—Dutch diplomat; at the beginning of the 18th century served at Dutch Embassy in Tur- key, carried out diplomatic missions for the Russian Government; in 1711-12 helped to conclude a peace treaty between Russia and Turkey.—65

Thiers, Louis Adolphe (1797-1877) — French historian and statesman, Prime Minister (1836, 1840), depu- ty to the Constituent (1848) and Legislative (1849-51) Assemblies; head of the Orleanists after 1848; organised the suppression of the Paris Commune (1871); President of the Republic (1871-73).—488

Thouvenel, Edouard Antoine (1818- 1866) — French diplomat, ambas- sador to Constantinople (1855-60), Foreign Minister (1860-62).—322- 23

Thurneyssen, Auguste—a director of the French joint-stock bank Crédit Mobilier.—358, 359

Thurneyssen, Charles—French banker, nephew of the above.— 358, 359

Timur (Tamerlane) (1336-1405) — Central Asian general and con- queror, founder of a large state in the East with Samarkand as its capital.—81, 557

Tippoo Saib (Tipu Sahib) (1750- 1799) —ruler of Mysore (1782-99); waged wars against British expan- sion in India in the 1780s and 1790s.—340

Tite, Sir William (1798-1873) — British architect and politician; Lib- eral, M.P. from 1855.—600

Todtleben (Totleben), Eduard Ivanovich, Count (1818-1884) —Rissian

Tooke, Thomas (1774-1858) —British economist, adherent of the classical school of political economy.— 382, 402 Toreno, José Maria Queipo de Llano, conde de (1786-1843)—Spanish politician of liberal leanings and historian, participant in the revolu- tions of 1808-14 and 1820-23; de- puty to the Cadiz Cortes, Minister of Finance (1834) and Prime Minis- ter (1835); lived in emigration from 1814 to 1820, 1823 to 1833 and from 1835.—625 Townshend, Charles, Viscount (1674- 1738) — British diplomat and politi- cian, at first Tory, later Whig; ambassador extraordinary and plenipotentiary to Holland (1709- 11), Secretary of State (1714-17, 1721-30).—56, 60, 65, 93

Tseang (Jiang Li'ang)—prefect in Luichow.—161, 162

(1796-1866) —French diplomat, Bonapartist, took part in the coup d'état of December 2, 1851; Foreign Minister (1851-52), ambas- sador to Spain (1853).—98

Turner, James Aspinall (1797-1867) — British cotton manufacturer and politician, Tory, M.P., President of the Manchester Commercial Associa- tion.—240

Valdez—Spanish general, participant in the revolution of 1854-56.—100, 101

**[p. 735]**

Vogorides, Stefan (Bogorov, Stojko) (c. 1775-c. 1862)—Turkish politician; Bulgarian by birth; Kaimakam of Moldavia (1821-22); father of Alexandros and Nikolaos Vog- orides.—324

Voltaire, François Marie Arouet (1694- 1778) — French philosopher, writer and historian of the Enlighten- ment.—309

Vorontsov, Mikhail Semyonovich, Prince (1782-1856)—Russian statesman, field marshal-general, in 1844-54 commander-in-chief of the troops in Transcaucasia and Governor- General in the Caucasus; after retir- ing lived in Odessa; Sidney Her- bert's maternal uncle.—249

Vsevolod III (1154-1212)—Grand Duke of Vladimir (1176-1212).—77

Wajid Ali Shah—King of Oudh (1847-56).—316, 529, 535

Walewski, Alexandre Florian Joseph Col- onna, comte (1810-1868) — French diplomat and statesman; son of Napoleon I and Polish Countess Marie Walewska; Foreign Minister (1855-60); was chairman at the Paris Congress (1856).—5, 470

Walker, William (1824-1860)— American adventurer; in the 1850s made several expeditions to Central American states with a view to uniting them in an empire under himself; seized power in Nicaragua in 1856 and declared himself Presi- dent; was taken prisoner and ex- ecuted in 1860, during his expedi- tion to Honduras.—162

Walpole, Horatio (Horace), Baron Wal- pole of Wolterton (1678-1757)—

Walpole, Sir Robert, Earl of Orford (1676-1745)—British statesman, Whig leader, Prime Minister (1721- 42); the first to form cabinets inde- pendent of the king and relying on the majority in Parliament; widely resorted to bribery; brother of the above.— 56, 57

Walpole, Spencer Horatio (Horace) (1806-1898) —British statesman, Tory, Home Secretary (1852, 1858- 59, 1866-67).—600

Wellesley, Richard Colley, Marquis (1760-1842)—British statesman, Governor-General of India (1797- 1805), Foreign Secretary (1809-12), Lord-Lieutenant of Ireland (1821- 28, 1833-34).—535 Wellington, Arthur Wellesley, Duke of (1769-1852)—British general and statesman, Tory; Prime Minister (1828-30), Foreign Secretary (De- cember 1834-April 1835).— 192, 468 Wheeler, Sir Hugh Massy (1789- 1857)—British general; in 1838-39 took part in the first Anglo-Afghan war, and in the Anglo-Sikh wars (1845-46, 1848-49); commander of the Cawnpore garrison (1856-57); took part in suppressing the nation- al liberation uprising of 1857-59 in India.—346-47, 367, 441, 442

Whiteside, James (1804-1876) —Irish lawyer and politician, Tory M.P.; Attorney-General (1858-59, 1866). —216

Whitlock, George (1798-1868)—British general; in 1818 entered the service of the East India Company; took part in suppressing the national liberation uprising of 1857-59 in India.—558, 582

William TV (1765-1837) —King of Great Britain and Ireland (1830- 37).—536

1855, during the Crimean war; M.P. (1856-59); commanded the garrison in Woolwich (1856-59).— 216, 447-48

Wilson, Sir Archdale (1803-1874) — British general; during the national liberation uprising in India com- mander of the troops which be- sieged and stormed Delhi (1857), and of the artillery during the siege of Lucknow (1858).—365, 370, 396, 398, 558

(d. 1857) —British colonel, took part in suppressing the nation Windham, Sir Charles Ash (1810- 1870) —British general; in 1854-56 fought in the Crimean war; com- manded British troops in Lahore (1857-61); took part in suppressing the national liberation uprising of 1857-59 in India.—442, 447-52,

Windham, William (1750-1810) — British statesman, Whig, subse- quently Tory, M.P., Secretary for War (1794-1801).—491, 495

Wood, Sir Charles, Viscount Halifax (1800-1885) —British statesman, Whig; Chancellor of the Exchequer (1846-52), President of the Board of Control for India (1852-55), First Lord of the Admiralty (1855-58), Secretary of State for India (1859- 66).—382

Woodburn—British general, in 1857 took part in suppressing the nation- al liberation uprising in India.— 316

Woronzoff, Prince—see Vorontsov, Mikhail Semyonovich, Prince

Wrangel, Friedrich Heinrich Ernst, Count von (1784-1877) — Prussian general, an active participant in the counter-revolutionary coup d'état in Berlin and in the dispersal of the Prussian National Assembly in November 1848.—107

Daniel (Bib.)—Old Testament prophet.— 296

Don Quixote—the title character in Cervantes' novel.—104

Zayas y Chacôn, José Pascual de (1772- 1827) — Spanish general, took part in the liberation war against Napoleonic France (1808-14) and in the revolution of 1820-23; deputy to the Cortes.— 630

Zimiskes—see John I Tzimisces

Zubov, Platon Alexandrovich, Prince (1767-1822) — Russian statesman, favourite of Catherine II.— 37

Falstaff, Sir John—a character in Shakespeare's tragedy King Henry IV and his comedy The Merry Wives of Windsor; a sly fat braggart and jester.— 240

Hector (Gr. Myth.) — a son of the Trojan King Priam; defender of Troy and one of the main charac- ters in Homer's Iliad; killed by Achilles.—154

Hercules (Heracles) (Gr. and Rom. Myth.) — son of Zeus; possessed enormous strength and courage.— 215, 229

Isàiah (Bib.)—Old Testament prophet.—95

**[p. 738]**

Macbeth—the title character in Shakespeare's tragedy.—486

Matthew (Bib.)—one of the four evangelists.—115, 191, 216 Nestor (Gr. Myth.) — a character in Homer's Iliad, the oldest and wisest of the Greek heroes who took part in the Trojan War.—154

Odysseus— a hero in the Iliad and the Odyssey who took part in the Tro- jan War; renowned for his courage and resource.—41

Polyphemus (Gr. Myth.)—a man-eating cyclop; blinded by Odysseus.—41

Samson (Bib.)—Old Testament hero known for his supernatural strength and courage.—46

Sindbad the Sailor—a hero in the Arabian Thousand and One Nights.— 248

Tartuffe—the title character in Molière's comedy.— 356

Timon—a character in Shakespeare's tragedy Timon of Athens.— 269

# Index oj Periodicals 



**[p. 762]**

INDEX OF PERIODICALS

Advertiser—see The Morning Advertiser

Allgemeine Zeitung—a conservative daily founded in 1798; from 1810 to 1882 it was published in Augsburg.— 502

L'Ambigu, variétés atroces et amusantes, journal dans le genre égyptien—a French- language newspaper published in London three times a month from 1803 to 1818.—493

L'Assemblée nationale—a Legitimist daily published in Paris from 1848 to 1857.—116

The Bengal Gazette—see The Calcutta Gazette

The Bombay Courier—an English-language newspaper, organ of the East India Company, founded in 1790; under this title it was published till 1861.— 376

The Bombay Gazette—an English-language daily published under this title from 1791 to 1914.—394

The Bombay Times—an English-language daily published under this title from 1838 to 1861.—318, 319

The Calcutta Gazette—an English-language newspaper published from 1784, official organ of the British Government.— 362

Chronicle—see The Morning Chronicle

Le Constitutionnel—a daily published in Paris from 1815 to 1817 and from 1819 to 1870; the mouthpiece of the monarchist bourgeoisie (Thiers party) during the 1848 revolution; a Bonapartist newspaper after the coup d'état of December 2, 1851.—115, 467, 470, 478, 515, 516, 568

The Continental Review—a weekly published in London in 1858 and 1859.— 480

Courrier Français de Londres.— 493

Daily Courant—a newspaper published in London from 1702 to 1735.—49

The Daily News—a liberal newspaper of the British industrial bourgeoisie; published under this title in London from 1846 to 1930.—163, 343, 345, 373, 388, 516

The Economist. Weekly Commercial Times, Bankers' Gazette, and Railway Monitor: a Political, Literary, and General Newspaper—organ of the big industrial bourgeoisie published in London since 1843.—121, 127, 130, 204, 383, 389, 390, 403, 415, 427, 444, 445, 461, 462, 500-02, 517-21, 560, 562, 564, 577

The Edinburgh Review, or Critical Journal—a liberal literary and political journal published in Edinburgh and London from 1802 to 1929; quarterly in the 1850s.—469

L'Etoile du Danube—a French-language liberal newspaper, published by Romanians in Brussels in 1856-57.—324-26

The Examiner—a liberal weekly published in London from 1808 to 1881.— 247

Le Figaro—a conservative daily published in Paris since 1854 as a continuation of Figaro, Journal nonpolitique that appeared in 1826-33; in the 1850s it took a Bonapartist stand.— 552

77ie Free Press. Journal of the Foreign Affairs Committees—a journal on questions of foreign policy, opposed to the Palmerston government; it was published by David Urquhart and his supporters in London from 1855 to 1865 (weekly till April 1858 and then monthly); it was renamed Diplomatic Review in 1866; it printed several works by Marx.—71, 389, 390, 425, 430, 540, 541

The Friend of China—see The Overland Friend of China

The Friend of India—an English newspaper founded in Serampore (Bengal) in 1818; in the 1850s it appeared once weekly and was liberal in policy.— 374, 376 Gaceta de Madrid—a Spanish newspaper founded in 1661, official organ of the government.—100, 286

Gazette nationale ou Moniteur universel—see Le Moniteur universel

The Globe and Traveller—a daily published in London from 1803 to 1921; mouthpiece of the Whigs until 1866, and later of the Conservatives.— 388, 443

Hamburger Correspondent—see Staats- und Gelehrten-Zeitung des Hamburgischen unpartheyischen Correspondenten

L'Indépendance belge. Journal mondial d'informations politiques et littéraires—a liberal

daily founded in Brussels in 1831.—137, 475, 476, 483

Journal de Madrid—a newspaper published in Madrid in 1855 and 1856.—103

Journal des Chemins de Fer, des Mines et des Travaux Publics—a bourgeois daily published in Paris from 1842.—132

Journal des Débats politiques et littéraires—a daily published in Paris from 1789 to 1944; organ of the moderate Orleanist opposition after the coup d'état of December 2, 1851.—15, 99, 101, 107, 456, 467

The Journal of Psychological Medicine and Mental Pathology—a quarterly published by doctor Forbes Winslow in London from 1848 to 1864; after some interval it appeared till 1883.—599

The Leader—a liberal weekly published in London from March 1850 to June I860.—103

Military Spectator—a weekly published in London in 1857 and 1858.— 374

The Mofussilite—an English-language weekly published in India; under this name it appeared from 1845 to the mid-1870s first in Meerut, then in Agra and Ambala.— 346

Le Moniteur universel—a daily published in Paris from 1789 to 1901 (under this name from 1811); from 1799 to 1869 it was an official government newspaper.—15-16, 18, 22, 23, 97, 118, 119, 122, 123, 130, 131, 133, 272, 273, 275, 276, 391, 413-15, 417, 454-57, 459, 463, 466, 472-75, 477, 478, 480, 482, 483, 490, 491, 493-501, 503, 516, 550, 551

The Morning Advertiser—a daily published in London from 1794 to 1934; in the 1850s it was a newspaper of the radical bourgeoisie.—249-50, 372, 596

The Morning Chronicle—a daily published in London from 1770 to 1862; organ of the Whigs in the 1840s, of the Peelites in the early 1850s, and later of the Conservatives.—388

The Morning Herald—a conservative daily published in London from 1780 to 1869.—104, 388

The Morning Post—a conservative daily published in London from 1772 to 1937; in the mid-nineteenth century it was the mouthpiece of the Right wing of the Whig Party grouped round Palmerston.—238, 247, 317, 328, 388, 596

Die Neue Zeit. Organ der Demokratie—a newspaper of German émigré workers published in London from June 1858 to April 1859.— 589

New-York Daily Tribune—a newspaper founded by Horace Greeley in 1841 and published until 1924; till the mid-1850s it was the mouthpiece of the Left wing of the American Whigs, then of the Republican Party; in the 1840s and 1850s it voiced progressive views and opposed slavery. Marx and Engels contributed to it from August 1851 to March 1862.—3-7, 10-12, 106, 107, 122, 151-55, 168, 207, 324, 360, 384, 541, 546

The New-York Herald—a daily newspaper of the Republican Party published from 1835 to 1924.—517

The North British Daily Mail—a Scottish newspaper founded in Glasgow in 1847, organ of the liberal bourgeoisie.—401

L'Opinione—an Italian daily founded in Turin in 1848; organ of Cavour's liberal monarchist party.—416

The Overland Friend of China—an English official newspaper published in Victoria (Hong Kong) from 1842 to 1859.—209

La Patrie—a daily published in Paris from 1841 to 1866; in 1850 it voiced the views of the Party of Order; after the coup d'état of December 2, 1851 it took a Bonapartist stand.—475, 516, 517, 520, 568

Le Pays. Journal de l'Empire—a daily founded in Paris in 1849, semi-official newspaper of Napoleon Ill's government from 1852 to 1870.— 320

Le Phare de la Loire—a French daily published in Nantes from 1815.—456

The Phoenix—an English-language newspaper published in Calcutta from 1856 to 1861.—446

Le Pouvoir—a Bonapartist daily founded in Paris in 1849; it was published under this title in 1850 and 1851.—516

The Press—a Tory weekly published in London from 1853 to 1866.— 247, 320

La Presse—a daily published in Paris in 1836-66; in the 1850s opposed the Second Empire, later organ of the Bonapartists.—102, 131

Punch, or the London Charivari—a comic weekly, liberal in policy; founded in London in 1841.—220, 472

Le Siècle—a daily published in Paris from 1836 to 1939; in the 1840s it voiced the views of that section of the petty bourgeoisie that demanded moderate constitutional reforms; in the 1850s it was a moderate republican newspaper.— 551

The Somerset County Gazette—a weekly founded in Taunton (Somerset, England) in 1836.—596

Somerset Gazette—see The Somerset County Gazette

Le Spectateur—the title under which the newspaper L'Assemblée nationale was published from September 1857 to January 1858.—456

Staats- und Gelehrten-Zeitung des Hamburgischen unpartheyischen Correspondenten—a monarchist daily published in Hamburg from 1814 to 1869.—498

The Standard—a conservative daily founded in London in 1827.— 388

The Sun—a liberal daily published in London from 1798 to 1876.— 249

The Times— a daily published in London since 1785.-8,9, 19, 112, 158-63,200,203,

2Ö5, 207-11, 215-17, 228, 230, 232, 239, 247, 249, 268, 269, 293, 294, 296, 298, 301, 306-09, 314-19, 321, 327, 330-33, 343, 345-46, 354-56, 359, 361, 364-66, 370, 376, 377, 381, 394, 400, 405-07, 410, 416, 423, 425, 426, 428, 435, 437, 443, 445, 449, 450, 456, 483, 493, 509, 510, 512, 515, 516, 527, 531, 533-34, 536, 546, 548-49, 552, 554, 556, 566-69, 570, 575, 576, 579, 582, 585-88, 590, 596, 597, 599, 601

The True Briton—a daily founded in London in 1792; in the early nineteenth century it was close to the government circles.—494

L'Union—a monarchist-clerical newspaper published under this title in Paris from 1848 to 1869.—568

L'Univers religieux, philosophique, politique, scientifique et littéraire—a clerical news- paper published in Paris from 1833 to 1860; in the 1850s it supported Napoleon III.—483, 515, 516, 551, 568

La Voix du Proscrit—a weekly of the Central Committee of European Democracy published under this title in Saint-Amand (France) from October 1850 to September 1851.—467

# Glossary of Geographical Names 



**[p. 777]**

GLOSSARY OF GEOGRAPHICAL NAMES '

Amoy Szeming Anatolia Asia Minor Araxes (river) Aras Astrabad Gurgan Aurungabad Aurangabad Austerlitz Slavkov Bahia Salvador Barrios Los Barrios Bassora Basra Boulogne Boulogne-sur Mer Braila Bräila British Guiana Guyana Bundelcund Bundelkhand Cabool, Caboul Kabul Canton Kwangchow Canton River Chu-Kiang

(Pearl River) Cattaro Kotor Cattaro, Bocche di Kotor, Gulf of Ceylon Sri Lanka Ching-Kiang- Foo Chinkiang Christiania Oslo Cilli Celje Citate Chetatea

Coleroon Cauvery Constantinople Istanbul Cracow Krakow Dantzic Gdansk Erivan Yerevan Erzeroum Erzerum or

Erzurum Fiumè Rijeka Friuli Udine Galatz Galaji Grisons Graubünden Gujerat Gujarat Hong Kong Hsiang-Kiang Illosfalva Illésfalva Inkermann Inkerman Ivangorod Dçblin Jassv Is§i Jaxartes Syr Darya Jericho Er Riha Kabardinsk Kabardinka Kamtchatka Kamchatka Karrachee Karachi Lambessa Lambèse Libau Liepaja Luy-chow-foo Luichow Macao Aomen Malghera Marghera

a This glossary includes geographical names occurring in Marx's and Engels' articles in the form customary in the press of the time but differing from the national names or from those given on modern maps. The left column gives geographical names as used in the original; the right column gives corresponding names as used on modern maps and in modern literature.
